name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
لَن تَنَالُوا۟ ٱلْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ (٩٢)
```

```english
You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you hold dear, and whatever you may spend of anything, Allah indeed knows it. (92)
```

```arabic
۞ كُلُّ ٱلطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ ۗ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ فَٱتْلُوهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٩٣)
```

```english
All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself before the Torah was sent down. Say, ‘Bring the Torah, and read it, if you are truthful.’ (93)
```

```arabic
فَمَنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٩٤)
```

```english
So whoever fabricates a lie against Allah after that—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (94)
```

```arabic
قُلْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ فَٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ (٩٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif, and he was not one of the polytheists. (95)
```

## (Commentary)

The connection of the first verse to what precedes it is not entirely clear. It is possible that it was not revealed alongside the rest of the verses, whose interconnectedness is beyond doubt. You have already encountered a similar issue regarding the timing of revelation in His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ تَعَالَوْا** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book, come...’" (3:64).

It is sometimes said that the address in the verse is directed toward the Children of Israel (بني إسرائيل) and continues to be so. The gist of the meaning—following the previous rebuking and blaming of them for their love of the world and their preference for wealth and possessions over the religion of Allah—is: You are untruthful in your claim of being related to Allah, the Glorified, and His prophets, and that you are people of righteousness and godwariness (التقوى). For you love the most precious of your possessions and are stingy in spending them; you only spend that which is mediocre and to which the soul is not attached—things whose loss or absence is of no consequence. This is despite the fact that righteousness (البر) cannot be attained except by a person spending what he loves from the most precious of his wealth, and its preservation is never missed by Allah, the Glorified. This is the summary of what has been said, but it contains an obvious forced interpretation (تمحل).

As for the rest of the verses, their connection to the preceding explanations is clear and beyond doubt.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَن تَنَالُوا الْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ** — **You will never attain piety until you spend out of what you hold dear** (3:92). "Attaining" (النيل) is reaching or arriving at something. Righteousness (البر) — *the expansive performance of good deeds* — is as al-Raghib stated: "Righteousness (البر) is the opposite of the sea (البحر). The concept of expansiveness was envisioned from it, and thus 'righteousness' was derived, meaning expansiveness in doing good." End quote.

His intent by "doing good" is broader than just internal acts, such as true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and pure intention (النية الطاهرة), or external acts of the limbs, such as worship (العبادة) of Allah and spending in the way of Allah, the Exalted. Both categories are included in His saying, the Exalted: **لَّيْسَ الْبِرَّ أَن تُوَلُّوا وُجُوهَكُمْ قِبَلَ الْمَشْرِقِ وَالْمَغْرِبِ وَلَٰكِنَّ الْبِرَّ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةِ وَالْكِتَابِ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ وَآتَى الْمَالَ عَلَىٰ حُبِّهِ ذَوِي الْقُرْبَىٰ وَالْيَتَامَىٰ وَالْمَسَاكِينَ وَابْنَ السَّبِيلِ وَالسَّائِلِينَ وَفِي الرِّقَابِ وَأَقَامَ الصَّلَاةَ وَآتَى الزَّكَاةَ وَالْمُوفُونَ بِعَهْدِهِمْ إِذَا عَاهَدُوا وَالصَّابِرِينَ فِي الْبَأْسَاءِ وَالضَّرَّاءِ وَحِينَ الْبَأْسِ** — **Piety is not to turn your faces to the east or the west; rather, piety is [personified by] those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day, the angels, the Book, and the prophets, and who give their wealth, for the love of Him, to relatives, orphans, the needy, the traveller and the beggar, and for [the freeing of] slaves, and maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and those who keep their pledges when they pledge, and those who are patient in adversity and hardship, and in the battlefront** (2:177).

By joining this verse with His saying: **لَن تَنَالُوا الْبِرَّ** (You will never attain righteousness...), it becomes clear that the intent is that spending wealth despite one's love for it is one of the pillars of righteousness (البر), which is not complete except by the gathering of all its components. Indeed, making spending the ultimate goal for attaining righteousness indicates a particular concern and emphasis on this specific part, due to the human instinct (غريزة) of heart-felt attachment to the wealth one has accumulated. Man considers it as if it were a part of his own self; if he loses it, it is as if he has lost a part of his own life's vitality—unlike other acts of worship and deeds where no such loss or disappearance from him is apparent.

From here, the error in the statement of some becomes clear—those who claimed that "righteousness" *is* the act of spending what you love. It is as if this speaker treated it like saying: "You will not be saved from the pain of hunger until you eat," and the like. However, this is refuted by the previously mentioned verse.

It also becomes clear from the aforementioned verse of *al-Baqarah* that the intent of righteousness (البر) is its literal linguistic meaning, namely expansiveness in good, for the verse explained it through the totality of creedal and practical goodness. From this, the weakness of the view of some becomes apparent: that righteousness refers to Allah's favor and blessing; as well as the view of others: that it refers to Paradise (الجنة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا تُنفِقُوا مِن شَيْءٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ بِهِ عَلِيمٌ** — **and whatever you spend, Allah indeed knows it** (3:92). This is a reassurance for the souls of the spenders that whatever they spend of the wealth dear to them will not go to waste without reward, for Allah, who commanded them to do so, is All-Knowing of their spending and what they spend.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُلُّ الطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ** — **All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself before the Torah was sent down** (3:93). Food (الطعام) refers to everything that is eaten and used for nourishment. Among the people of the Hijaz, it was used specifically for wheat (البُر), and their minds would turn to it when the term was used absolutely. Lawfulness (الحل) is the opposite of prohibition (الحرمة); it seems to be derived from "untying" (الحل) as opposed to "tying" (العقد) or "binding" (العقل), thus conveying the meaning of being released or permitted. Israel (إسرائيل) is the Prophet Jacob (يعقوب), peace be upon him (عليه السلام). He was named so because he was a striver for the sake of Allah and victorious through Him. The People of the Book say its meaning is "the victor who prevailed over Allah," because he wrestled with Allah in a place called Peniel and defeated Him (according to the Torah)—this is among the things the Quran deems false and which reason (العقل) considers impossible.

His saying: **إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ** (except what Israel had forbidden himself) is an exception from the "food" mentioned just before. His saying: **مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ** (before the Torah was sent down) relates to the verb "was" (كان) in the first clause. The meaning is: Allah did not prohibit any food for the Children of Israel before the revelation of the Torah, except what Israel had prohibited for himself.

In His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِالتَّوْرَاةِ فَاتْلُوهَا إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ** — **Say, ‘Bring the Torah and read it, if you are truthful’** (3:93), there is an indication that they used to deny this—meaning the lawfulness of all food for them before the Torah. This is further evidenced by the fact that they used to deny abrogation (النسخ) — *the replacement of one religious ruling with another* — in divine laws and considered it impossible, as previously mentioned in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **مَا نَنَسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنْسِهَا** — "Any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten..." (2:106). Naturally, they would deny His saying, the Exalted: **فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَاتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ** — "Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We prohibited them certain good things that were permitted to them" (4:160).

Similarly, His saying afterward: **قُلْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ فَاتَّبِعُوا مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا** — **Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif’** (3:95), indicates that they were using what they denied (regarding the lawfulness of all food for them before the Torah, and that the prohibition was only sent down upon them due to their wrongdoing as an abrogation of lawfulness with prohibition) as a means to cast doubt upon the Muslims. They used it to object to what the Messenger of Allah, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, was reporting from his Lord: that his religion is the creed of Abraham, the *Hanif* (الحنيف) — *the primordial monotheistic faith that inclines toward truth and away from all falsehood* — which is a natural creed (ملة فطرة) containing neither excess nor deficiency. For how could it be otherwise when they used to say that Abraham was a Jew following the law of the Torah? How then could his creed include the lawfulness of what the Torah prohibited, given that abrogation is not permissible?

Thus, it has become clear that the verse is concerned with repelling a doubt raised by the Jews. It appears from the fact that the verse does not explicitly quote the doubt from them—as the Quran usually does in most instances, such as His saying: **وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ يَدُ اللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ** — "The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied’" (5:64), and His saying: **وَقَالُوا لَن تَمَسَّنَا النَّارُ إِلَّا أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَةً** — "And they say, ‘The Fire shall not touch us except for a number of days’" (2:80), and His saying: **وَقَالُوا قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌ** — "And they say, ‘Our hearts are uncircumcised’" (2:88), among many other verses.

This is also supported by His saying several verses later: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ مَنْ آمَنَ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Why do you bar those who have faith from the way of Allah...’" until He says: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِن تُطِيعُوا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَانِكُمْ كَافِرِينَ** — "O you who have faith! If you obey a group of those who were given the Book, they will turn you back into faithless ones after your having found faith" (3:99-100).

In short, it appears from this that it was a doubt the Jews were casting not upon the Messenger of Allah, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, but upon the believers during their meetings and conversations.

The gist of it was: How can the Prophet be truthful when he reports abrogation (النسخ), and that Allah only prohibited good things for the Children of Israel because of their wrongdoing? This would be an abrogation of a prior lawfulness, which they claimed is not permissible for Allah, the Glorified; rather, prohibited things are prohibited eternally without the possibility of changing Allah's judgment. The summary of the response from the Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, by the teaching of Allah the Exalted, is: The Torah itself speaks of all food being lawful before its revelation; so bring the Torah and read it if you are truthful in your claim. This is His saying: **كُلُّ الطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ** (All food was lawful to the Children of Israel...) until His saying: **إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ** (if you are truthful).

If you refuse to bring the Torah and read it, then admit that you are the ones fabricating lies against Allah and that you are the wrongdoers (الظالمون). That is His saying: **فَمَنِ افْتَرَىٰ** (So whoever fabricates...) until His saying: **ظَالِمُونَ** (wrongdoers).

It has thereby become clear that I am truthful in my call; so follow my creed, which is the creed of Abraham, a *Hanif*. That is His saying: **قُلْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ فَاتَّبِعُوا مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ** (Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham...’) to the end of the verse.

The commentators have various explanations for clarifying the meaning of the verse, but in any case, they mentioned that the verse addresses a doubt raised by the Jews related to abrogation, as mentioned.

The most peculiar thing said in this regard is what some mentioned: that the verse addresses a doubt raised by the Jews regarding abrogation, the formulation of which was: The Jews supposedly said, "If you, O Muhammad, are upon the creed of Abraham and the prophets after him—as you claim—then how do you permit what was prohibited for him and them, such as camel meat? Since you have permitted what was prohibited for them, you should not claim to be a confirmer of them or in agreement with their religion, nor should you single out Abraham by saying: 'I am the most worthy of him.'"

The summary of the response [according to this view] is: All food was lawful for people in general, including the Children of Israel, but the Children of Israel prohibited things for themselves by the sins and evil deeds they committed, as He said: **فَبِظُلْمٍ مِّنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا حَرَّمْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ طَيِّبَاتٍ أُحِلَّتْ لَهُمْ** — "Due to the wrongdoing of the Jews, We prohibited them certain good things that were permitted to them" (4:160). Thus, the intent of "Israel" is the people of Israel, as is used among them, not Jacob alone. The meaning of their prohibiting it for themselves is that they committed wrongdoing and earned sins, which became the cause for the prohibition. The phrase **مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ** (before the Torah was sent down) relates to His saying: **حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ** (Israel had forbidden). If the intent of "Israel" were Jacob himself, then the phrase "before the Torah was sent down" would be redundant and superfluous speech, as it is self-evident that Jacob lived long before the Torah, so there would be no reason to mention it.

This is the summary of what he mentioned. Another person mentioned something similar, except he said: The intent of the Children of Israel prohibiting it for themselves is their prohibiting it through their own legislation without it being based on a revelation from Allah, the Glorified, to one of their prophets, just as the Arabs of the *Jahiliyyah* (الجاهلية) — *the period of ignorance before Islam* — used to do, as Allah recounted in His Book.

Both of them have engaged in a level of forced interpretation (التكلف) that no expert would accept, taking the speech out of its proper course. The main thing that drove them to this was their interpreting His saying: **مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ التَّوْرَاةُ** (before the Torah was sent down) as relating to His saying: **حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ** (Israel had forbidden), whereas it actually relates to His saying: **كَانَ حِلًّا** (was lawful) at the beginning of the sentence, and the phrase **إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ** (except what [Israel] had forbidden) is an parenthetical exception.

From this, it becomes clear that there is no need to take "Israel" to mean the Children of Israel, as they imagined, based on the supposed lack of soundness in the meaning otherwise.

Furthermore, while using "Israel" to mean the Children of Israel is permissible—similar to how they say "Bakr," "Taghlib," "Nizar," and "Adnan" meaning the sons of Bakr, Taghlib, Nizar, and Adnan—its occurrence regarding the Children of Israel is a usage not known among the Arabs at the time of revelation. Nor did the Quran follow this path with this word (outside of this instance they claim), despite the Children of Israel being mentioned in nearly forty places; among them is this very verse: **كُلُّ الطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَائِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ** (All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had forbidden himself). What, according to their view, is the difference between the two instances in the verse? Why would they be referred to first as "the Children of Israel" and then immediately followed by "Israel," especially since this is a context most prone to confusion? It is enough to note that the vast majority of commentators understood it to mean Jacob, not his sons.

One of the best proofs that it refers to Jacob is His saying: **عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ** (for himself), using the singular masculine pronoun referring back to Israel. If the intent were the Children of Israel, it would have been necessary to say "for itself" (على نفسها) or "for themselves" (على أنفسهم).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ فَأْتُوا بِالتَّوْرَاةِ فَاتْلُوهَا إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ** — **Say, ‘Bring the Torah and read it, if you are truthful’** (3:93). That is, so the truth may become clear as to which of the two parties is in the right: I or you. This is a response provided by Allah, the Exalted, to His Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنِ افْتَرَىٰ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ** — **So whoever fabricates a lie against Allah after that, it is they who are the wrongdoers** (3:94). Its apparent meaning is that it is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, addressing His Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. Based on this, it contains a reassurance for the soul of the Prophet, صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم, that his enemies among the Jews are the wrongdoers (الظالمون) after this clarification of their fabrication of lies against Allah. It is also an allusion toward the Jews, and the speech follows the course of metonymy (الكناية).

As for the possibility of the speech being a continuation of the Prophet's words, it is not suited by the apparent singular form of the demonstrative address in His saying: **مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ** (after that). On this basis as well, the speech follows the course of metonymy and veiling the defeated opponent so that the speech may be accepted, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّا أَوْ إِيَّاكُمْ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى أَوْ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ** — "Indeed either we or you are rightly guided or in manifest error" (34:24). The thing pointed to by "that" (ذلك) is the clarification and the proof.

He only said: **مِن بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ** (after that)—even though the fabricator is a wrongdoer in any case—because wrongdoing (الظلم) is not fully realized until after clarification, as has been said. The restriction in His saying: **فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ** (it is they who are the wrongdoers) is a restriction of reversal (قصر قلب) in any case.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ فَاتَّبِعُوا مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ حَنِيفًا** — **Say, ‘Allah has spoken the truth; so follow the creed of Abraham, a Hanif’** (3:95). That is: Since the truth is with me in what I have informed you of and called you toward, then follow my religion and admit the lawfulness of camel meat and other good things that Allah has permitted. He had only prohibited them for you as a punishment for your transgression and wrongdoing, as He, the Exalted, informed.

So His saying: **فَاتَّبِعُوا** (so follow...) is like a metonymy for following his religion. He did not mention it explicitly because they were already confessing to the creed of Abraham. This serves as an indication that what he calls to of religion is *Hanif* (حنيف) and natural (فطري), because the primordial nature (الفطرة) does not prevent man from eating good meats and other provisions.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi* and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "When Israel ate camel meat, it would aggravate a pain in his side, so he prohibited camel meat for himself, and that was before the Torah was sent down. When the Torah was sent down, he did not prohibit it, nor did he eat it."

**I say:** Something close to this is narrated through the chains of the *Ahl al-Sunnah wa al-Jama'ah*.

In the narration, the phrase "he did not prohibit it, nor did he eat it"—the two subject pronouns refer to Moses, as indicated by the context. The meaning is: Moses did not prohibit it, nor did he eat it. It is also possible that "did not eat it" (لم يأكله) comes from the form of "feeding" (التأكيل), meaning to allow or enable eating. It appears from *al-Taj* that the second and third verbal forms carry the same meaning here.
